column7: COMMENTARY ON THE BOOK OF RUTH. Argument. The genealogy of the Patriarch Judah is here carried through from his son Phares down to David, from whom Christ was born — to demonstrate (as the whole of Holy Scripture has Christ as its mark and end) that Christ was born from Judah, as Jacob had promised and foretold in blessing his son (Gen. 49:10). Into this genealogy there is introduced Ruth, a Moabitess and Gentile, but who by a marvellous chance — or rather by God's providence — entered it equally with Rahab the Canaanite, as St. Jerome explains: "to show that he who came to blot out the sins of all was not ashamed to be born of sinners; and that in Ruth the Gentile, the ancestress of Christ, the future calling of the Gentiles and the exclusion of the Jews was prefigured." Tropologically: that in Ruth an ideal and model of the pious, modest, and holy wife should be given. Symbolically: to indicate that nobility of birth is given by God, who took Ruth, a base and poor Gentile, and enrolled her in the family of Abraham and Judah, making her the ancestress of David and of Christ. Hence Ruth is called by the Rabbis, by St. Chrysostom, and by Blessed Peter Damian: "Mother of the kingdom and of kings." For all the kings of Judah descended from her. This book is an appendix to Judges, to which the Hebrews attach it; its history took place under the Judges (ch. 1:1). It is a prelude and introduction to the book of Kings, which describes the deeds of David and the other kings descended from Ruth. The book's authorship is uncertain; some hold Hezekiah, others Ezra, but most likely Samuel. Commentators include Rupert, Abulensis, Lyrannus, Hugo, Dionysius, Cajetanus, Serarius, and Fevardentius.

IN THE DAYS OF ONE OF THE JUDGES, WHEN THE JUDGES RULED, THERE WAS A FAMINE IN THE LAND. — Lapide examines which judge presided. (1) Josephus, Zonara, and the Master of the Histories Scholasticae hold it was Heli the Pontiff — but Lapide rejects this: Heli judged 40 years, and Samuel and Saul another 40; David succeeded immediately at age 30. The generations Boaz → Obed → Jesse → David could not have been accomplished in the 80 years of Heli, Saul, and Samuel. (2) The Rabbis and Lyrannus hold it was Abesan (identifying him with Boaz) — but this is impossible, since Abesan became judge in the year 212 after the capture of Jericho, when Salmon married Rahab; if Boaz = Abesan, Boaz would have been about 272 years old at the time of Ruth, which was impossible in that age. (3) Seder Olam holds it was Ehud the second judge. (4) Abulensis thinks it was Barak. (5) Most aptly, Lapide's confrere Salianus holds that the famine occurred under Abimelech son of Gideon, and the marriage of Boaz and Ruth under his successor Thola. The famine was sent by God to punish the parricides and idolatry of Abimelech and the Shechemites.

BOAZ — A MIGHTY MAN OF GREAT WEALTH. — Hebrew gibbor chail: "strong man" — robust in body and soul, or powerful in riches (which chail also signifies, and which is more apt here, since Boaz was being prepared as a husband, in whom wealth is chiefly regarded). His name in Hebrew means "in strength" or "in him is strength." The two columns of Solomon's Temple were called Jachin and Boaz (3 Kgs. 7:21) — that is, direction and fortitude (Septuagint, 2 Par. 3:17). The Author of the Incomplete Work (hom. 4 in Matt.) interprets Boaz as "prevailing in strength." Lapide also draws the lesson for estate-owners: Boaz visiting his fields and workers teaches lords to visit their properties and labourers often — "Under this fear the steward and the household will be in their duty" (Cato, De Re Rustica).

I WILL SEEK REST FOR THEE, THAT IT MAY BE WELL WITH THEE. — That is, Naomi will seek for Ruth an honourable and wealthy marriage, in which, free from the labours of poverty, she may rest in a rich and gentle husband's care and live quietly and happily. Aben-Ezra: "Girls are not accustomed to rest until they are married." Plutarch calls marriage "as it were the port of youth" — lest by wandering lusts it make shipwreck of chastity.

BOAZ WENT UP TO THE GATE AND SAT DOWN THERE. — Judges sat at the gate so that country-people and strangers could easily approach them. On the unnamed kinsman called in Hebrew peloni almoni ("such a one"): Lapide notes that his name is from pele ("hidden and wonderful") and alman ("silent, dumb") — his name is deliberately suppressed as unworthy of record, since he refused to raise up seed and name for his dead kinsman. Serarius: "His name is justly buried in eternal oblivion who permits the name of his brother and kinsman to be buried in the same way."
